coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots from roasted Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar L.): Formation, biodistribution and cytotoxicity
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yukun
Wu, Yanyang
Wang, Haitao
Liu, Shan
Song, Liang
Li, Shen
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: The endogenous carbon quantum dots (CQDs) produced during food processing have potential uncertainty to human health. The objective of this study was to investigate the formation, biodistribution and cytotoxicity of CQDs in roasted Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar Linnaeus). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis showed that the CQDs were mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. The morphology, functional groups and optical properties were highly dependent on the roasting time. In vivo experiments in mice demonstrated that the CQDs distributed in the digestive tract, kidney, liver, and even brain, which indicated that they could cross the blood–brain barrier. The cell imaging results indicated that the CQDs could readily gain access to the normal rat kidney (NRK) cells, and caused autophagosome formation. The proportion of live cells decreased to 34.62% at 6 mg mL−1 of CQDs, and the energy generation route was changed from aerobic to glycolytic metabolism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Salmo salar L.
Biodistribution
Flow cytometric
Energy metabolism

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of CQDs from roasted Salmo salar L.
Characterization of Salmo salar L. CQDs
In vitro biodistribution in live cells
Western blot analysis and immunofluorescence assay of autophagic marker protein
Flow cytometric analysis of the cell cycle and cell apoptosis
Structural characterization of Salmo salar L. CQDs
In vivo biodistribution of the Salmo salar L. CQDs in mice
In vitro biodistribution in live cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology characterization of the Salmo salar L. CQDs. TEM images of CQD-10 (a), CQD-20 (b), CQD-30 (c), CQD-40 (d), CQD-50 (e) and CQD-60 (f). Insets show the high-resolution TEM images and corresponding particle size distribution histograms of CQDs. CQD-10, CQD-20, CQD-30, CQD-40, CQD-50 and CQD-60 represent the CQDs extracted from roasted Salmo salar L. at 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 min, respectively.

Biodistribution of the Salmo salar L. CQDs in major organs. (a) Ex vivo imaging of major organs of the BALB/c mice oral administration with Salmo salar L. CQDs at a dose of 2 g kg−1 body weight at 0, 0.5, 2, 6 and 24 h. (b) Relative fluorescence intensity of major organs. a,bDifferent letters in a column indicate significant difference (p < 0.05) (ANOVA).

Cell imaging of the Salmo salar L. CQDs. Bright field and fluorescence microscope images of NRK cells incubated with Salmo salar L. CQDs for 6 h. Bright field images of NRK cells at 3 (a) 6 (e) and 0 (i) mg mL−1. 405 nm excitation wavelength fluorescence images of NRK cells at 3 (b) 6 (f) and 0 (j) mg mL−1. Overlay images of NRK cells at 3 (c) 6 (g) and 0 (k) mg mL−1. Enlarged of overlay images of NRK cells at 3 (d) 6 (h) and 0 (l) mg mL−1. Scale bar = 50 μm.

Western blot analysis and immunofluorescence assay of autophagic marker protein. Immunoblot analysis of LC3 (a) and endogenous SQSTM1/p62 (c) conversion induced by the CQDs at the concentration of 3 (CQD-3) and 6 (CQD-6) mg mL−1 for 6 h. Quantitative of LC3-II (b) and SQSTM1/p62 (d) levels by densitometric analysis relative to β-actin. Cell immunofluorescence assay for NRK cells without treatment (e) or with (f) 3 (CQD-3) and (g) 6 (CQD-6) mg mL−1 CQDs treatment for 6 h. (h) Number of autophagosomes per cell (n = 10) were counted and presented graphically. Different lower case letters indicate significant difference at p < 0.05.

Flow cytometric analysis of the cell cycle and cell apoptosis. Flow cytometry result of NRK cells (a) untreated or treated with (b) 3 mg mL−1, (c) 6 mg mL−1 Salmo salar L. CQDs; (d) 3 mg mL−1, and (e) 6 mg mL−1 glucose CDs (G-CDs) with Annexin V-FITC and PI staining method (Annexin V-FITC binding versus PI uptake). (f) Relative proportion of apoptosis cells treated with CQDs at different concentrations. CQD-3 and CQD-6 represent 3 and 6 mg mL−1 of Salmo salar L. CQDs, respectively. Glucose CDs were used as a positive control. G-CD-3 and G-CD-6 represent 3 and 6 mg mL−1 of glucose CDs, respectively.

Mitochondrial and glycolysis stress analysis. (a) ECAR plots of NRK cells upon exposure to Salmo salar L. CQDs and glucose CDs (positive control) at 6 mg mL−1 using the blank NRK cells as a negative control. OM: oligomycin, 2-DG: 2-deoxy-D-glucose (b) Indices of glycolytic pathway activation, calculated from ECAR profile: basal glycolysis (Glyc), glycolytic (Glyc) capacity and glycolytic (Glyc) reserve. (c) Metabolic profile of the stimulatory effect of Salmo salar L. and glucose CDs (G-CDs) on oxidative phosphorylation and glycolysis. (d) The stressed OCR and ECAR of glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots from roasted Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar L.): Formation, biodistribution and cytotoxicity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Qinggongyuan 1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology
National Engineering Research Center of Seafood

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Qinggongyuan 1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Endocrinology, Dalian Municipal Central Hospital of Dalian Medical University, Dalian 116033, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dalian Municipal Central Hospital of Dalian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Endocrinology, Dalian Municipal Central Hospital of Dalian Medical University, Dalian 116033, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The endogenous carbon quantum dots (CQDs) produced during food processing have potential uncertainty to human health. The objective of this study was to investigate the formation, biodistribution and cytotoxicity of CQDs in roasted Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar Linnaeus). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis showed that the CQDs were mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. The morphology, functional groups and optical properties were highly dependent on the roasting time. In vivo experiments in mice demonstrated that the CQDs distributed in the digestive tract, kidney, liver, and even brain, which indicated that they could cross the blood–brain barrier. The cell imaging results indicated that the CQDs could readily gain access to the normal rat kidney (NRK) cells, and caused autophagosome formation. The proportion of live cells decreased to 34.62% at 6 mg mL−1 of CQDs, and the energy generation route was changed from aerobic to glycolytic metabolism.

ce:section.ce:para: Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar L.) is an important seafood, not only for its taste and culinary qualities, but also for its health properties, with regard to its minerals, vitamins, high-quality proteins with essential amino acids, astaxanthin and fatty acids such as omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, particularly eicosapentaenoic acid, docosahexaenoic acid (Costa et al., 2015). The roasted Salmo salar L. is a popular seafood due to its delicious taste and uniquearoma flavor (Ovissipour, Rasco, Tang, & Sablani, 2017). The chemical toxins, such as acrylamide and heterocyclic amines formed in the roasted food, have a positive association with an elevated health risk to the consumer (Chen, Kao, Chen, Huang, & Chen, 2013; Lee et al., 2016). In addition, recent works have reported the endogenous carbon nanoparticles produced in roasted foods, such as the amorphous carbon nano-structures in various food caramels (Sk, Jaiswal, Paul, Ghosh, & Chattopadhyay, 2012), and the carbon dots (CDs) generated from overcooked barbecue meat (Wang et al., 2013). Fluorescent nanoparticles were also reported in roasted pike eel (Bi et al., 2017), grilled hamburger (Li et al., 2017), and baked lamb (Wang et al., 2017). It is noteworthy that carbon per se is not considered as toxic element. However, exposure to carbon nanostructures in roasted food is of particular concern, as they have unique properties that can be associated with toxicity, such as high surface to volume ratio, photocatalytic activity and enhanced permeability (Nel, Ruoslahti, & Meng, 2017). This is hardly mentioned in the literatures, and our knowledge about the endogenous nanoparticles from roasted food is insufficient.

Carbon nanoparticles have been reported to have certain adverse effects on biological systems (Nel et al., 2015). The metabolic stress in human stem cells was reported to be caused by the carbon nanoparticles derived from bakery products (Al-Hadi, Periasamy, Athinarayanan, & Alshatwi, 2016). The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could induce cell death, apoptosis and autophagy when they were used as photodynamic agents (Markovic et al., 2012). The CQD-induced reactive oxygen species were found to lead to apoptosis, autophagy, and inflammatory response in THP-1 activated macrophages (Qin et al., 2015). Moreover, the CQDs could suppress proinflammatory T-cell responses through the way of autophagy-dependent induction of tolerogenic monocyte-derived dendritic cells. (Tomiä et al., 2017) To date, the presence of CQDs in roasted foods and their biosafety have not been sufficiently investigated. We have no idea how about the formation of Salmo salar L. CQDs during the roasting process, and whether the CQDs are potentially toxic.

In this work, the formation of Salmo salar L. CQDs was reported for various time periods at 200 °C and the CQD morphology, size, nanostructure, optical properties and functional groups were characterized. By taking the CQDs generated at 200 °C for 50 min as an example, in vitro and in vivo biodistribution, and cytotoxicity were studied in the normal rat kidney (NRK) cell lines. Notably, we found that the Salmo salar L. CQDs can be absorbed and cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB) presenting in the brain of mice. Moreover, the cellular toxicity was investigated by analysis of the cell cycle, apoptosis, autophagy and cell respiration. The hazard assessment of the endogenous CQDs produced in roasted Salmo salar L. provided important evidence for their potential adverse effects.

Atlantic Salmo salar L. fillets were purchased from the local market in Dalian, China. Glucose was purchased from Beijing Soledad Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The macroporous resin D101 and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) grade methanol were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC (St. Louis, MO, USA). Dulbecco's modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS), were purchased from Biological Industries (BioInd; Kibutz Beit Ha’Emek, Israel). Anti-LC3 antibody and anti-SQSTM1 antibody for western blot analysis were obtained from Cell Signaling Technology (Danvers, MA, USA). β-actin purchased from Santa Cruz Biotechnology (Santa Cruz, CA). Hoechst 33342 was obtained from Aladdin Reagent Inc., China. Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) conjugated antirabbit antibody was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membranes were purchased from Millipore (Burlington, MA). Chemiluminescence reagent for protein bands visualization was obtained from Millipore Co. (Burlington, MA, USA). Cell cycle and Annexin V-FITC/propidium iodide (PI) apoptotic detection kits were obtained from Shanghai Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cell glycolysis stress and energy phenotype test kits were used to measure oxygen consumption rate (OCR) and extracellular acidification rate (ECAR) and were purchased from Agilent Technologies, Inc. (Santa Clara, CA, USA). The normal rat kidney (NRK) cell line is kindly donated by Li Yu lab of Tsinghua University. All other chemicals and solvents used in this study were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of CQDs from roasted Salmo salar L.
ce:section.ce:para: Raw Salmo salar L. fillets (500 g) were cut into pieces of approximately 10 × 10 × 10 mm and roasted in an electric heating oven (Lecon, Po2pt, Guangdong, China) for 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 min at 200 °C. The roasted Salmo salar L. samples were mixed with 1,500 mL ethanol (100%) (1:3, w/v) and ultrasonically extracted for 120 min. After filtration and removal of impurities, the filtrate was evaporated to remove the solvent by vacuum rotary evaporation, and then redissolved in 150 mL of deionized water and washed three times with 50 mL of chloroform to remove the hydrophobic phase. To further purify the sample, the concentrated aqueous solution was added to a macroporous resin D101 column and the fluorescent fractions were collected. The resulting fluorescent fraction containing Salmo salar L. CQDs was further purified with an Elite preparative HPLC instrument consisting of a model UV230Ⅱ detector (Elite Co., Ltd., Dalian, China) with an elution SinoChrom ODS-BP column (20 mm × 300 mm, 10 μm) at 25 °C using 10% v/v methanol as mobile phase at a flow rate of 18 mL min−1 for 120 min. The fluorescent fraction with a retention time in the range of 12–120 min was collected and freeze-dried for further used. Glucose CDs were artificially synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of 0.5 g of glucose in 15 mL of water at 180 °C for 4 h. The purification procedure of the glucose CDs was the same as that of Salmo salar L. CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Salmo salar L. CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield (QY) was performed according to procedures described in previous studies (Bi et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017).

Fifteen male BALB/c mice (18–22 g) were equally divided into five groups (n = 3) purchased from Dalian Medical University. Four groups were orally administered with a single dose of Salmo salar L. CQDs at 2 g kg−1 body weight (200 μL) to evaluate the biodistribution of CQDs, while the control group mice were orally administrated with a 0.9% NaCl solution (200 μL). The mice from each group that received the CQDs were sacrificed at 0.5, 2, 6, and 24 h. All the protocols for all animal procedures were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Dalian Polytechnic University.

Major organs/tissue, including small intestine, vermiform appendix, colorectum, stomach, spleen, kidneys, liver, heart, lung and brain, were harvested and imaged on an multi-functional in vivo imaging system (MIIS) (Molecular Device Corporation, Sunnyvale, CA, USA). Spectral fluorescent images were recorded with an appropriate filter for the CQDs. Exposure time was 200 ms for all the measurements. The small intestine, liver, brain and kidney were collected and frozen at −80 °C after sacrificing the mice that had been orally administered with the CQDs. Then these organs were sectioned into 10 μm slices for histological analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biodistribution in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: NRK cells were seeded in a laser focusing dish at an initial cell density of 2 × 105 cells mL−1 in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS, penicillin (100 units mL−1) and streptomycin (100 μg mL−1). After incubation in 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h, the NRK cells were treated with CQDs from Salmo salar L. roasted for 50 min at a concentration of 0, 3 and 6 mg mL−1, separately, for 6 h at 37 °C. All the subsequent experiments were performed using the CQDs from Salmo salar L. roasted for 50 min as an example. After washing three times with PBS to remove free Salmo salar L. CQDs, cellular imaging was performed with an SP8 confocal laser fluorescence microscope (Leica Microsystems GmbH., Wetzlar, Germany) with excitation laser of 405 nm and an emission wavelength within 425–600 nm.

The effect of Salmo salar L. CQDs on cell growth in real time was monitored with xCELLigence system (ACEA Biosciences, San Diego, California, USA) using glucose CDs as a control. The NRK cells seeded in a 16-well E-plate at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well were cultured for 6 h. The medium in each well was changed to 150 μL of DMEM containing Salmo salar L. CQDs at concentrations of 0, 1, 3 and 6 mg mL−1, respectively, and the NRK cells in DMEM with glucose CDs were used as positive control. The data recording frequency was every 15 min and the RTCA software was used for data acquisition and analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Western blot analysis and immunofluorescence assay of autophagic marker protein
ce:section.ce:para: The NRK cells incubated with 3 and 6 mg mL−1 Salmo salar L. CQDs for 6 h were lysed with radioimmuno precipitation assay buffer. The extracts were separated by a 12% sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE), and placed on a PVDF membrane. Then the PVDF membrane was blocked with 5% nonfat dry milk in a mixture of tris-buffered saline containing 0.1% tween 20 (TBST) at room temperature for 1 h, and incubated with primary antibody at 4 °C overnight. After thoroughly washing with TBST buffer, the PVDF membrane was incubated with HRP-conjugated secondary antibody for 1 h. Immunolabeling was imaged with an enhanced chemiluminescence using β-actin as a control.

For autophagic marker protein assay, the NRK cells were treated with 3 and 6 mg mL−1 Salmo salar L. CQDs for 6 h, then washed three times with PBS and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min. After permeabilization with 0.5% Triton-X 100 for 15 min, the NRK cells were blocked with 5% FBS for 1 h and incubated with LC3 antibody overnight at 4 °C. Finally, FITC labelled secondary antibody was added to the NRK cells and incubated for 1 h at room temperature. Fluorescent images were acquired on an SP8 Leica confocal laser fluorescence microscope (Microsystems GmbH., Wetzlar, Germany) by staining the cellular nuclei with Hoechst for 15 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometric analysis of the cell cycle and cell apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: Cell cycle and cellular apoptosis analysis were performed with the NRK cells seeded at 2 × 105 cells mL−1 in a 12-well plate. After incubation in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h, the NRK cells were incubated with Salmo salar L. CQDs at a concentration of 3 and 6 mg mL−1, respectively, using glucose CDs (3 and 6 mg mL−1) as a positive control. The NRK cells were cultured for 6 h and harvested by trypsinization and centrifugation at 1,000 rpm for 5 min, washing thoroughly with 1 mL of PBS. For the cell cycle assay, the collected cells were fixed in 70% precooled ethanol for 12 h at 4 °C. After centrifugation, the cell pellets were suspended with precooled PBS and further treated with RNase and PI for 30 min at 37 °C. The number of cells was measured on a FACSVerse flow cytometer (Becton, Dickinson and Company, Franklin Lakes, NJ, USA). For the apoptosis assay, the collected cells were stained with Annexin V-FITC and PI for 10 min and submitted for flow cytometry assay.

The oxygen consumption rate (OCR) and extracellular acidification rate (ECAR) of cells were measured with a Seahorse XF-8 extracellular flux analyzer (Seahorse Bioscience, Billerica, MA, USA) to assess the cellular mitochondrial and glycolysis stress upon treatment the Salmo salar L. CQDs. Briefly, the NRK cells seeded in XF 8-well cell culture plate (Seahorse Bioscience) at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well for 12 h were treated with 6 mg mL−1 Salmo salar L. CQDs and glucose CDs for 6 h. Then the cells were transferred to a CO2-free incubator for 1 more hour. Initially, the NRK cells were cultured in a sugar-free medium for 15 min to consume the substrate, followed by adding the concentrated glucose solution, which was catabolized via the glycolytic pathway to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP), nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide, water and protons. The ECAR value increased significantly at 21 min which was defined as basal glycolytic capacity. Subsequently, upon injection of oligomycin, the mitochondrial ATP production was restrained, and the increased ECAR value indicated the maximal glycolytic capacity. Finally, a glucose analog, 2‑deoxy‑D‑glucose (2-DG) that inhibits glycolysis through competitive binding to glucose hexokinase was added. The decrease in the ECAR values confirmed that the glycolysis stress occurred. The glycolytic reserve was defined by the difference between maximal glycolytic capacity and glycolysis. For the ECAR analysis, glucose (10 mM), oligomycin (1 μM) and 2-deoxyglucose (5 μM) were added into cell medium at 14, 35 and 54 min, repsetively. For the energy phenotype, FCCP (0.5 μM) and oligomycin (1 μM) were added at 14 min. Measurements were reported in pmol min−1 for the OCR and mpH min−1 for the ECAR.

A one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with three measurements was used to evaluate differences between the signal among the tested samples and controls. All the statistics analysis was conducted with a commercial software from Statistical Product and Service Solutions (SPSS) Inc. (Version 19.0, Chicago, Illinois, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of Salmo salar L. CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The roasted Salmo salar L. is a kind of favourite seafood and the formation and hazardous effects of CQDs may have a great impact on its consumption due to their potential safety concern. In this work, the CQDs were found for the first time in Salmo salar L. roasted at 200 °C for various periods of time (10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 min) and more experimental details can be found in the support information. TEM images (Fig. 1 a–f) reveal that the CQDs are well dispersed, with a particle size that follows a normal distribution in the range of 1.0–5.0 nm. The average size of Salmo salar L. CQDs, based on the calculation of about 100 particles derived from Salmo salar L. roasted for 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 min, was 3.72 ± 0.34, 3.52 ± 0.51, 3.19 ± 0.58, 3.08 ± 0.70 2.64 ± 0.42 and 2.46 ± 0.51 nm, respectively. In addition, the average size of Salmo salar L. CQDs decreased with the extension of the roasting time. Also, clear lattice fringes are observed after roasting for 30–60 min, corresponding to a spacing value of 0.18 nm after 30 and 40 min, and 0.21 nm after 50 and 60 min, respectively. This may be attributed to the sp2 (1120) graphitic crystal phase of graphene (Alam, Park, Ghouri, Park, & Yong, 2015), suggesting the formation of CQDs through a continuous heating after 30 min. A proposed hypothesis is that the myofibrillar proteins in Salmo salar L. might be degraded at 200 °C, resulting in the formation of fluorescent polymer chains (FPCs), which are amorphous before 20 min of roasting as other researchers have previously reported (Qu, Wang, Lu, Liu, & Wang, 2012). When the roasting time was more than 30 min, CQDs with clear lattice fringes were formed, which is consistent with our previous findings in roasted meat (Wang et al., 2017). In particular, the lattice spacing increased from 0.18 to 0.21 nm (indicated by white arrows) with the extension of the roasting time. This increase may result from the formation of the graphitic structure which are consistent with the (1 0 5) diffraction planes of graphitic carbons (JCPDS card 26–1076) and the (1 0 3) diffraction plane of the diamond-like (sp3) carbon (JCPDS cards 26–1081 and 26–1083). The formation of these Salmo salar L. CQDs is similar to the preparation of artificial CQDs by the hydrothermal method, in which natural polymer starch is pyrolyzed or carbonized to form nanostructures (Chen et al., 2018; Lu, Bruheim, Haugsgjerd, & Jacobsen, 2014).

The typical XRD analysis (Fig. S1) shows that the Salmo salar L. CQDs produced at various time periods have similar patterns with a broader diffraction peak at around 22.0° that is assigned to highly disordered carbon structures (Qu et al., 2012). Similar result was also observed in the one-step green synthesis of CQDs from cabbage (Alam et al., 2015). In addition, the CQDs have similar FT-IR spectra, indicating that they have similar structural composition (Fig. S2). The strong absorption at 3,265 cm−1 could be ascribed to OH stretching and the band at 3,055 cm−1 is attributed to NH stretching vibration. The characteristic absorption at 2,810 cm−1 was due to CH (stretching). The peak at 1,680 cm−1 was ascribed to the amide I band, and the peaks at 1,575 and 1,480 cm−1 corresponded to the amide II and III bands from the amide NH bending and CN stretching vibrations (Jiang et al., 2015). The FTIR analysis revealed the presence of various groups on the Salmo salar L. CQDs surface.

In the XPS spectra of the Salmo salar L. CQDs (Fig. S3a), C1s, O1s and N1s peaks were found around 285, 532 and 399 eV, respectively, suggesting that the CQDs are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements (Li, Lau, Tang, Ji, & Yang, 2014). Also, in the high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. S3b), three peaks at 284.9, 286.4 and 288.6 eV are attributed to the exitance of CC/CC, CO/CN and CO/CN bonds, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. S3c) reveal the presence of deconvoluted peaks at 532.3 and 532.3 eV due to CO and CO groups, respectively. The N1s spectra (Fig. S3d) can be decomposed into two peaks at around 400 and 401 eV, which are attributed to the CN and NH groups, respectively (Chen, Jin, Wu, Tung, & Tang, 2015). As shown in Table S1, the XPS analysis reveals that with the increase of the roasting time the C content increases from 61.23% to 82.63% with an associated O content that decreases from 25.71 to 14.60%, while the N content decreases from 12.39% to 2.56%. The increase in the ratio of carbon element suggested that the carbonization degree increased with the increase of the roasting time.

Like the artificially synthesized CQDs, the Salmo salar L. CQDs showed strong fluorescence. The ultraviolet–visible spectra showed that all the Salmo salar L. CQDs exhibited a broad absorption band around 240 nm (Fig. S4a–f) due to the π-π* electronic transitions of the aromatic CC molecules (Alam et al., 2015). In addition, an excitation-dependent emission behavior was also observed, and the maximum excitation wavelength red-shifted from 340 to 400 nm, as the roasting time increased. The emission change of the Salmo salar L. CQDs in the 0–20 min period underwent the most red-shifting compared with that in the 30–40 min and 50–60 min periods. This revealed that the Salmo salar L. CQDs produced after a longer roasting time are more stable, and no obvious red-shifting behavior was observed. Moreover, the fluorescence QY of CQD-10, CQD-20, CQD-30, CQD-40, CQD-50 and CQD-60 was 2.21, 2.97, 3.23, 8.75 13.76 and 12.09%, respectively, with corresponding fluorescence lifetime of 5.81, 6.03, 6.25, 6.29, 6.43 and 6.64 ns. Thy slight change of the lifetime indicated that the roasting time has a slight effect on the fluorescence lifetime of the Salmo salar L. CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution of the Salmo salar L. CQDs in mice
ce:section.ce:para: By taking the Salmo salar L. CQDs generated at 200 °C for 50 min as an example, their biodistribution was evaluated in mice to determine their fate after oral administration. The fluorescence signal reached its maximum value 0.5 h for small intestine and stomach, and 2, 6 and 6 h after administration for kidney, liver and brain, respectively (Fig. 2 a). However, no fluorescence enhancement was observed in lung. Remarkably, the CQDs were also observed in the brain, suggesting that they can cross the BBB, particularly due to their ultra-small sizes and multi-functionalization on surface (Cox et al., 2018), thus drawing more attention to their potential effects on the brain. No obvious fluorescence enhancement was found in heart and spleen (not shown). After 24 h oral administration, the fluorescence signal in the major organs decreased to a very low level, indicating that the Salmo salar L. CQDs were possibly eliminated from the mouse body within this time period (Fig. 2b). These results strongly suggested that the Salmo salar L. CQDs were able to be absorbed by the digestive system and delivered with body fluid like blood and lymph into the above-mentioned organs. The ex vivo fluorescence imaging at histological level (Fig. S5) further confirmed this above-described results. Strong fluorescence of the CQDs was observed in small intestine, and the fluorescence intensity of the Salmo salar L. CQDs reached its maximum value 0.5 h after oral administration. Histological analysis also revealed significant (p < 0.05) fluorescence in the kidney tissues at 0.5 and 2 h after oral administration of the CQDs, indicating that the Salmo salar L. CQDs are excluded from the kidneys. In the liver and brain tissue samples, a maximum value of the CQD fluorescence signal was observed at 6 h after administration. This result concided well with that of the ex vivo fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biodistribution in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: Since significant fluorescence enhancement was observed in kidney, the normal rat kidney cells were selected for evaluation of the biodistribution of the Salmo salar L. CQDs. After incubation for 6 h with a Salmo salar L. CQD concentration of 3 mg mL−1, the NRK cells emitted brilliant blue light upon the irradiation of 405 nm, indicating that the Salmo salar L. CQDs had crossed the live cells, and entered both the cell membrane and cytoplasm (Fig. 3 c, 3d). Moreover, at a concentration of 6 mg mL−1, a fluorescence signal was observed within the nucleus (Fig. 3h). This finding indicated that the Salmo salar L. CQDs distribution within the cells changed with the increase of their concentration and the CQDs were transferred from the cytoplasm to nucleus at higher concentrations. The concentration effect on the distribution of the Salmo salar L. CQDs in the cell is quite different from what we reported previously in the cell cytoplasm (Li, Jiang, Wang, Cong, & Tan, 2018). In contrast to the untreated control, the NRK cells treated with 3 and 6 mg mL−1 of Salmo salar L. CQDs showed slight cellular shrinking, which is a feature of apoptotic cells (Mirshafiee et al., 2018).

The potential cytotoxicity of Salmo salar L. CQDs to NRK cells was evaluated with an impedance-based RTCA system using the synthesized glucose CDs as a control. The dynamic changes of the cell index (CI) after exposure to CQDs at concentrations of 0, 1, 3 and 6 mg mL−1 (Fig. S6). The CI values represent the electronic impedance changes after calculation of the electronic signal as cells attach (live) or detach (dead) from the sensor (Moniri et al., 2015). The decreased CI value means that the addition of a cytotoxic agent caused cell detachment. It is noteworthy that there was no CI decline for cells exposed to CQDs concentration of 1 mg mL−1 or less (Fig. S6a). However, when the CQD concentration was more than 3 mg mL−1, the general trend was that the CI value decreased with the increase of the CQD concentration, indicating their toxic effect on the NRK cells. In particular, the higher Salmo salar L. CQD concentration of 6 mg mL−1 caused a significant decline in the CI after 6 h of treatment. However, at the same concentration, the glucose CDs led to a faster CI decline after 1 h treatment. After incubation for 10 h, the Salmo salar L. CQDs at the concentration of 3 and 6 mg mL−1, exhibited similar cytotoxic effect to that of the glucose CDs at a concentration of 6 mg mL−1 (Fig. S6b). Since the CQDs with concentration<1 mg mL−1 did not cause adverse effect to the cells, the 3 and 6 mg mL−1 of CQDs and 6 h cells exposure were selected for the subsequent experiments.

Autophagy and apoptosis are completely different biological processes involving completely different proteins and mechanisms (Ma et al., 2015). Autophagy is the natural, regulated mechanism of the cell that disassembles dysfunctional components, involving a protein degradation system in which ubiquitinated protein complexes and organelles are sequestered and digested by lysosomal hydrolases (Rathinam, Vanaja, & Fitzgerald, 2012). The microtubule associated protein 1 light chain 3 beta with symbol MAP LC3-II, a lapidated form of the cytosolic MAP LC3-I, is a strong hallmark for the autophagosome membrane formation. In Fig. 4 a, the level of LC3-II expression was found to be significantly higher in NRK cells exposed to 3 mg mL−1 Salmo salar L. CQDs compared with the untreated controls (p < 0.05), indicating the induction of the autophagosome formation (Li et al., 2014). However, although the level of LC3-II induced by 6 mg mL−1 of Salmo salar L. CQD was higher than that in the control, the increase was not statistically significant (Fig. 4b), probably due to the induction of cell death. Additionally, the sequestosome 1 (SQSTM1/p62) was also monitored as a protein selectively degraded by autophagy (Itakura & Mizushima, 2011). The level of SQSTM1/p62 was markedly increased after incubation with 3 mg mL−1 CQDs for 6 h (Fig. 4c), which is indicative of autophagosome formation and corresponding with the increase in LC3-II protein (Ha, Weitzmann, & Jr, 2014). However, the increase of the level of SQSTM1/p62 by 6 mg mL−1 Salmo salar L. CQDs treatment was not significant (Fig. 4d). In addition to evidence the autophagy process, the immunofluorescence assay result showed that the number of autophagosomes by 3 mg mL−1 CQDs treatment (Fig. 4f) was dramatically increased in punctate structures compared with control (Fig. 4e, 4 g) by counting the number of punctate fluorescence signal (Fig. 4h). This indicated autophagosome formation (Mizushima, Yoshimori, & Levine, 2010).

The early effect of cell apoptosis, autophagy or necrosis can be evaluated in cell cycle progression. Arrest in the Gap0/Gap1 (G0/G1), DNA synthesis (S), or in Gap2/mitosis (G2/M) phase and irreversible damage can lead to cell apoptosis (Asharani, Mun, Hande, & Valiyaveettil, 2009). As shown in Fig. S7, after incubation with CQDs for 6 h, the proportion of cells in the G1 phase increased, whereas a decrease in the proportion in the S phase was observed compared to the untreated cells. After treatment with 3 and 6 mg/mL of Salmo salar L. CQDs, 63.71 and 61.35% of cells were in G0/G1, respectively, whereas 52.73% of the untreated cells were in G0/G1. The portion of cells in the S phase at 3 and 6 mg/mL Salmo salar L. CQDs were 20.14% and 18.81%, respectively, which was clearly smaller than that of the control (29.19%). The proportion of cells in the G2/M phase for any concentration of Salmo salar L. CQDs was approximately the same as that in the control group. Irreversible damage can lead to cell apoptosis, resulting in the accumulation of cells in a sub-G1 phase (Asharani et al., 2009). In comparison, the glucose CDs induced about a 10% increase in the percentage of cells in the G1 phase, thus leading to cell apoptosis.

Generally, the energy metabolism plays an important role in determining the essential cellular functions and any adverse stimulation might cause changes to this vital process. To evaluate the impact of CQDs on the energy metabolism, the mitochondrial respiration and glycolysis assay was conducted by monitoring the value changes of ECAR and OCR. Upon the addition of glucose, the ECAR value in cells treated with Salmo salar L. CQDs was increased slightly compared with that in the control cells, and almost no increase in the cells treated with glucose CDs was observed (Fig. 6 a). Also, both the basal glycolysis and maximal glycolytic capacity of the cells (Fig. 6b) significantly decreased after CQD addition, as suggested by the significant decrease in the ECAR value upon adding oligomycin to inhibit mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS). The cells treated with glucose CDs showed a greater decline in the ECAR parameters, indicating a serious inhibition of glycolytic function at 6 mg/mL (Ma et al., 2015).

Cellular energy is generated via ATP by OXPHOS in the mitochondria and by glycolysis in the cytoplasm (Wang et al., 2017). The two energy supply routes occur at different ratios when the cells suffer from an impairment due to the added toxicants. The effect of Salmo salar L. CQDs on the metabolism of NRK cells can be illustrated by plotting the OCR versus ECAR (Fig. 6c). The NRK cells treated with the Salmo salar L. CQDs were operating at approximately the same ECAR level but decreased OCR level compared to the control. The stressed OCR (%) affected by Salmo salar L. CQDs was higher than that of the control, but not significantly higher than that of the stressed ECAR (%) (Fig. 6d). This finding indicated that the CQDs preferentially changed the energy production route from aerobic to glycolytic metabolism (Zhang et al., 2012). On the other hand, the positive control, glucose CDs, stimulated both glycolysis and aerobic respiration, suggesting a different toxic effect.

In summary, different roasting time led to production of Salmo salar L. CQDs with different particle size, surface groups, and fluorescence property. The in vitro cell imaging results revealed that the CQDs can enter into the cytoplasmic and nuclear regions of the NRK cells, while in vivo biodistribution revealed that they were absorbed in the small intestine, and present in the mouse brain. The NRK cells treated with 3 mg mL−1 CQDs induced autophagy and autophagosome formation. The CQDs preferentially induced the change of energy generation from aerobic to glycolytic metabolism. The hazardous evaluation of Salmo salar L. CQDs demonstrated that much effort is needed in understanding of their interaction and potential impact on the health of humans.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key R & D Program of China (2017YFD0400103) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872915).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2019.05.017.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
